name: pnpm
description: Fast, disk space efficient package manager

subcommands.name: add
subcommands.description: Installs a package and any packages that it depends on. By default, any new package is installed as a production dependency

name: --offline
options.description: If true, pnpm will use only packages already available in the store. If a package won't be found locally, the installation will fail

name: --prefer-offline
options.description: If true, staleness checks for cached data will be bypassed, but missing data will be requested from the server. To force full offline mode, use --offline

name: --ignore-scripts
options.description: Do not execute any scripts defined in the project package.json and its dependencies

name: --reporter
options.description: Allows you to choose the reporter that will log debug info to the terminal about the installation progress

name: -P
--save-prod
options.description: Install the specified packages as regular dependencies

name: -D
--save-dev
options.description: Install the specified packages as devDependencies

name: -O
--save-optional
options.description: Install the specified packages as optionalDependencies

name: --no-save
options.description: Prevents saving to `dependencies`

name: -E
--save-exact
options.description: Saved dependencies will be configured with an exact version rather than using pnpm's default semver range operator

name: --save-peer
options.description: Using --save-peer will add one or more packages to peerDependencies and install them as dev dependencies

name: --ignore-workspace-root-check
-W#
options.description:
Adding a new dependency to the root workspace package fails, unless the --ignore-workspace-root-check or -W flag is used.
For instance, pnpm add debug -W

name: --global
-g
options.description: Install a package globally

name: --workspace
options.description: Only adds the new dependency if it is found in the workspace

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

subcommands.name: install
subcommands.description:
Pnpm install is used to install all dependencies for a project.
In a CI environment, installation fails if a lockfile is present but needs an update.
Inside a workspace, pnpm install installs all dependencies in all the projects.
If you want to disable this behavior, set the recursive-install setting to false

subcommands.name: install-test
subcommands.description: Runs pnpm install followed immediately by pnpm test. It takes exactly the same arguments as pnpm install

name: --offline
options.description: If true, pnpm will use only packages already available in the store. If a package won't be found locally, the installation will fail

name: --prefer-offline
options.description: If true, staleness checks for cached data will be bypassed, but missing data will be requested from the server. To force full offline mode, use --offline

name: --ignore-scripts
options.description: Do not execute any scripts defined in the project package.json and its dependencies

name: --reporter
options.description: Allows you to choose the reporter that will log debug info to the terminal about the installation progress

name: -P
--save-prod
options.description:
Pnpm will not install any package listed in devDependencies if the NODE_ENV environment variable is set to production.
Use this flag to instruct pnpm to ignore NODE_ENV and take its production status from this flag instead

name: -D
--save-dev
options.description: Only devDependencies are installed regardless of the NODE_ENV

name: --no-optional
options.description: OptionalDependencies are not installed

name: --lockfile-only
options.description: When used, only updates pnpm-lock.yaml and package.json instead of checking node_modules and downloading dependencies

name: --frozen-lockfile
options.description: If true, pnpm doesn't generate a lockfile and fails to install if the lockfile is out of sync with the manifest / an update is needed or no lockfile is present

name: --use-store-server
options.description: Starts a store server in the background. The store server will keep running after installation is done. To stop the store server, run pnpm server stop

name: --shamefully-hoist
options.description: Creates a flat node_modules structure, similar to that of npm or yarn. WARNING: This is highly discouraged

subcommands.name: update
subcommands.description:
Pnpm update updates packages to their latest version based on the specified range.
When used without arguments, updates all dependencies. You can use patterns to update specific dependencies

name: --recursive
-r
options.description: Concurrently runs update in all subdirectories with a package.json (excluding node_modules)

name: --latest
-L
options.description: Ignores the version range specified in package.json. Instead, the version specified by the latest tag will be used (potentially upgrading the packages across major versions)

name: --global
options.description: Update global packages

name: -P
--save-prod
options.description: Only update packages in dependencies and optionalDependencies

name: -D
--save-dev
options.description: Only update packages in devDependencies

name: --no-optional
options.description: Don't update packages in optionalDependencies

name: --interactive
-i
options.description: Show outdated dependencies and select which ones to update

name: --workspace
options.description:
Tries to link all packages from the workspace. Versions are updated to match the versions of packages inside the workspace.
If specific packages are updated, the command will fail if any of the updated dependencies are not found inside the workspace. For instance, the following command fails if express is not a workspace package: pnpm up -r --workspace express

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: remove
subcommands.description: Removes packages from node_modules and from the project's package.json

name: --recursive
-r
options.description:
When used inside a workspace, removes a dependency (or dependencies) from every workspace package.
When used not inside a workspace, removes a dependency (or dependencies) from every package found in subdirectories

name: --global
options.description: Remove a global package

name: -P
--save-prod
options.description: Only remove the dependency from dependencies

name: -D
--save-dev
options.description: Only remove the dependency from devDependencies

name: --save-optional
-O
options.description: Only remove the dependency from optionalDependencies

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: link
subcommands.description: Makes the current local package accessible system-wide, or in another location

name: --dir
-C
options.description: Changes the link location to <dir>

name: --global
options.description: Links the specified package (<pkg>) from global node_modules to the node_nodules of package from where this command was executed or specified via --dir option

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: unlink
subcommands.description:
Unlinks a system-wide package (inverse of pnpm link).
If called without arguments, all linked dependencies will be unlinked.
This is similar to yarn unlink, except pnpm re-installs the dependency after removing the external link

name: --recursive
-r
options.description: Unlink in every package found in subdirectories or in every workspace package, when executed inside a workspace

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: import
subcommands.description: Pnpm import generates a pnpm-lock.yaml from an npm package-lock.json (or npm-shrinkwrap.json) file

subcommands.name: rebuild
subcommands.description: Rebuild a package

name: --recursive
-r
options.description: This command runs the pnpm rebuild command in every package of the monorepo

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: prune
subcommands.description: Removes unnecessary packages

name: --prod
options.description: Remove the packages specified in devDependencies

name: --no-optional
options.description: Remove the packages specified in optionalDependencies

subcommands.name: fetch
subcommands.description: EXPERIMENTAL FEATURE: Fetch packages from a lockfile into virtual store, package manifest is ignored: https://pnpm.io/cli/fetch

name: --prod
options.description: Development packages will not be fetched

name: --dev
options.description: Only development packages will be fetched

subcommands.name: patch
subcommands.description: This command will cause a package to be extracted in a temporary directory intended to be editable at will

name: --edit-dir
options.description: The package that needs to be patched will be extracted to this directory

subcommands.name: patch-commit
subcommands.description: Generate a patch out of a directory

subcommands.name: audit
subcommands.description:
Checks for known security issues with the installed packages.
If security issues are found, try to update your dependencies via pnpm update.
If a simple update does not fix all the issues, use overrides to force versions that are not vulnerable.
For instance, if lodash@<2.1.0 is vulnerable, use overrides to force lodash@^2.1.0.
Details at: https://pnpm.io/cli/audit

name: --audit-level
options.description: Only print advisories with severity greater than or equal to <severity>

name: --fix
options.description: Add overrides to the package.json file in order to force non-vulnerable versions of the dependencies

name: --json
options.description: Output audit report in JSON format

name: --dev
-D
options.description: Only audit dev dependencies

name: --prod
-P
options.description: Only audit production dependencies

name: --no-optional
options.description: Don't audit optionalDependencies

name: --ignore-registry-errors
options.description: If the registry responds with a non-200 status code, the process should exit with 0. So the process will fail only if the registry actually successfully responds with found vulnerabilities

subcommands.name: list
subcommands.description:
This command will output all the versions of packages that are installed, as well as their dependencies, in a tree-structure.
Positional arguments are name-pattern@version-range identifiers, which will limit the results to only the packages named. For example, pnpm list "babel-*" "eslint-*" semver@5

name: --recursive
-r
options.description: Perform command on every package in subdirectories or on every workspace package, when executed inside a workspace

name: --json
options.description: Log output in JSON format

name: --long
options.description: Show extended information

name: --parseable
options.description: Outputs package directories in a parseable format instead of their tree view

name: --global
options.description: List packages in the global install directory instead of in the current project

name: --depth
options.description:
Max display depth of the dependency tree.
pnpm ls --depth 0 will list direct dependencies only. pnpm ls --depth -1 will list projects only. Useful inside a workspace when used with the -r option

name: --dev
-D
options.description: Only list dev dependencies

name: --prod
-P
options.description: Only list production dependencies

name: --no-optional
options.description: Don't list optionalDependencies

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

subcommands.name: outdated
subcommands.description: Checks for outdated packages. The check can be limited to a subset of the installed packages by providing arguments (patterns are supported)

name: --recursive
-r
options.description: Check for outdated dependencies in every package found in subdirectories, or in every workspace package when executed inside a workspace

name: --long
options.description: Print details

name: --global
options.description: List outdated global packages

name: --no-table
options.description: Prints the outdated dependencies in a list format instead of the default table. Good for small consoles

name: --compatible
options.description: Prints only versions that satisfy specifications in package.json

name: --dev
-D
options.description: Only list dev dependencies

name: --prod
-P
options.description: Only list production dependencies

name: --no-optional
options.description: Doesn't check optionalDependencies

subcommands.name: why
subcommands.description: Shows all packages that depend on the specified package

name: --recursive
-r
options.description: Show the dependency tree for the specified package on every package in subdirectories or on every workspace package when executed inside a workspace

name: --json
options.description: Log output in JSON format

name: --long
options.description: Show verbose output

name: --parseable
options.description: Show parseable output instead of tree view

name: --global
options.description: List packages in the global install directory instead of in the current project

name: --dev
-D
options.description: Only display the dependency tree for packages in devDependencies

name: --prod
-P
options.description: Only display the dependency tree for packages in dependencies

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: run
subcommands.description: Runs a script defined in the package's manifest file

name: -r
--recursive
options.description: This runs an arbitrary command from each package's "scripts" object. If a package doesn't have the command, it is skipped. If none of the packages have the command, the command fails

name: --if-present
options.description: You can use the --if-present flag to avoid exiting with a non-zero exit code when the script is undefined. This lets you run potentially undefined scripts without breaking the execution chain

name: --parallel
options.description: Completely disregard concurrency and topological sorting, running a given script immediately in all matching packages with prefixed streaming output. This is the preferred flag for long-running processes over many packages, for instance, a lengthy build process

name: --stream
options.description: Stream output from child processes immediately, prefixed with the originating package directory. This allows output from different packages to be interleaved

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: exec
subcommands.description:
Execute a shell command in scope of a project.
node_modules/.bin is added to the PATH, so pnpm exec allows executing commands of dependencies

name: -r
--recursive
options.description:
Execute the shell command in every project of the workspace.
The name of the current package is available through the environment variable PNPM_PACKAGE_NAME (supported from pnpm v2.22.0 onwards)

name: --parallel
options.description: Completely disregard concurrency and topological sorting, running a given script immediately in all matching packages with prefixed streaming output. This is the preferred flag for long-running processes over many packages, for instance, a lengthy build process

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: test
subcommands.description:
Runs an arbitrary command specified in the package's test property of its scripts object.
The intended usage of the property is to specify a command that runs unit or integration testing for your program

subcommands.name: start
subcommands.description:
Runs an arbitrary command specified in the package's start property of its scripts object. If no start property is specified on the scripts object, it will attempt to run node server.js as a default, failing if neither are present.
The intended usage of the property is to specify a command that starts your program

subcommands.name: publish
subcommands.description:
Publishes a package to the registry.
When publishing a package inside a workspace, the LICENSE file from the root of the workspace is packed with the package (unless the package has a license of its own).
You may override some fields before publish, using the publishConfig field in package.json. You also can use the publishConfig.directory to customize the published subdirectory (usually using third party build tools).
When running this command recursively (pnpm -r publish), pnpm will publish all the packages that have versions not yet published to the registry

name: --tag
options.description: Publishes the package with the given tag. By default, pnpm publish updates the latest tag

name: --dry-run
options.description: Does everything a publish would do except actually publishing to the registry

name: --ignore-scripts
options.description: Ignores any publish related lifecycle scripts (prepublishOnly, postpublish, and the like)

name: --no-git-checks
options.description: Don't check if current branch is your publish branch, clean, and up-to-date

name: --access
options.description: Tells the registry whether the published package should be public or restricted

name: --force
options.description: Try to publish packages even if their current version is already found in the registry

name: --report-summary
options.description: Save the list of published packages to pnpm-publish-summary.json. Useful when some other tooling is used to report the list of published packages

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

subcommands.name: recursive
subcommands.description: Runs a pnpm command recursively on all subdirectories in the package or every available workspace

options.description: Install a package globally

options.description: Only adds the new dependency if it is found in the workspace

subcommands.description: Runs a script defined in the package's manifest file

name: -r
--recursive
options.description: This runs an arbitrary command from each package's "scripts" object. If a package doesn't have the command, it is skipped. If none of the packages have the command, the command fails

name: --if-present
options.description: You can use the --if-present flag to avoid exiting with a non-zero exit code when the script is undefined. This lets you run potentially undefined scripts without breaking the execution chain

name: --parallel
options.description: Completely disregard concurrency and topological sorting, running a given script immediately in all matching packages with prefixed streaming output. This is the preferred flag for long-running processes over many packages, for instance, a lengthy build process

name: --stream
options.description: Stream output from child processes immediately, prefixed with the originating package directory. This allows output from different packages to be interleaved

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: exec
subcommands.description:
Execute a shell command in scope of a project.
node_modules/.bin is added to the PATH, so pnpm exec allows executing commands of dependencies

name: -r
--recursive
options.description:
Execute the shell command in every project of the workspace.
The name of the current package is available through the environment variable PNPM_PACKAGE_NAME (supported from pnpm v2.22.0 onwards)

name: --parallel
options.description: Completely disregard concurrency and topological sorting, running a given script immediately in all matching packages with prefixed streaming output. This is the preferred flag for long-running processes over many packages, for instance, a lengthy build process

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json

subcommands.name: test
subcommands.description:
Runs an arbitrary command specified in the package's test property of its scripts object.
The intended usage of the property is to specify a command that runs unit or integration testing for your program

subcommands.name: publish
subcommands.description:
Publishes a package to the registry.
When publishing a package inside a workspace, the LICENSE file from the root of the workspace is packed with the package (unless the package has a license of its own).
You may override some fields before publish, using the publishConfig field in package.json. You also can use the publishConfig.directory to customize the published subdirectory (usually using third party build tools).
When running this command recursively (pnpm -r publish), pnpm will publish all the packages that have versions not yet published to the registry

name: --tag
options.description: Publishes the package with the given tag. By default, pnpm publish updates the latest tag

name: --dry-run
options.description: Does everything a publish would do except actually publishing to the registry

name: --ignore-scripts
options.description: Ignores any publish related lifecycle scripts (prepublishOnly, postpublish, and the like)

name: --no-git-checks
options.description: Don't check if current branch is your publish branch, clean, and up-to-date

name: --access
options.description: Tells the registry whether the published package should be public or restricted

name: --force
options.description: Try to publish packages even if their current version is already found in the registry

name: --report-summary
options.description: Save the list of published packages to pnpm-publish-summary.json. Useful when some other tooling is used to report the list of published packages

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

name: --link-workspace-packages
options.description:
Link locally available packages in workspaces of a monorepo into node_modules instead of re-downloading them from the registry. This emulates functionality similar to yarn workspaces.
When this is set to deep, local packages can also be linked to subdependencies.
Be advised that it is encouraged instead to use npmrc for this setting, to enforce the same behaviour in all environments. This option exists solely so you may override that if necessary

name: --workspace-concurrency
options.description: Set the maximum number of tasks to run simultaneously. For unlimited concurrency use Infinity

name: --bail
options.description: Stops when a task throws an error

name: --no-bail
options.description: Don't stop when a task throws an error

name: --sort
options.description: Packages are sorted topologically (dependencies before dependents)

name: --no-sort
options.description: Disable packages sorting

name: --reverse
options.description: The order of packages is reversed

name: --filter
options.description:
Filtering allows you to restrict commands to specific subsets of packages.
pnpm supports a rich selector syntax for picking packages by name or by relation.
More details: https://pnpm.io/filtering

args.name: Filepath / Package
args.description: To only select packages under the specified directory, you may specify any absolute path, typically in POSIX format

subcommands.name: server
subcommands.description: Manage a store server

subcommands.name: start
subcommands.description: Starts a server that performs all interactions with the store. Other commands will delegate any store-related tasks to this server

name: --background
options.description: Runs the server in the background, similar to daemonizing on UNIX systems

name: --network-concurrency
options.description: The maximum number of network requests to process simultaneously

name: --protocol
options.description: The communication protocol used by the server. When this is set to auto, IPC is used on all systems except for Windows, which uses TCP

name: --port
options.description: The port number to use when TCP is used for communication. If a port is specified and the protocol is set to auto, regardless of system type, the protocol is automatically set to use TCP

name: --store-dir
options.description: The directory to use for the content addressable store

name: --lock
options.description: Set to make the package store immutable to external processes while the server is running or not

name: --no-lock
options.description: Set to make the package store mutable to external processes while the server is running or not

name: --ignore-stop-requests
options.description: Prevents you from stopping the server using pnpm server stop

name: --ignore-upload-requests
options.description: Prevents creating a new side effect cache during install

subcommands.name: stop
subcommands.description: Stops the store server

subcommands.name: status
subcommands.description: Prints information about the running server

subcommands.name: store
subcommands.description: Managing the package store

subcommands.name: status
subcommands.description:
Checks for modified packages in the store.
Returns exit code 0 if the content of the package is the same as it was at the time of unpacking

subcommands.name: add
subcommands.description:
Functionally equivalent to pnpm add,
except this adds new packages to the store directly without modifying any projects or files outside of the store

subcommands.name: prune
subcommands.description:
Removes orphan packages from the store.
Pruning the store will save disk space, however may slow down future installations involving pruned packages.
Ultimately, it is a safe operation, however not recommended if you have orphaned packages from a package you intend to reinstall.
Please read the FAQ for more information on unreferenced packages and best practices.
Please note that this is prohibited when a store server is running

subcommands.name: path
subcommands.description: Returns the path to the active store directory

subcommands.name: init
subcommands.description: Creates a basic package.json file in the current directory, if it doesn't exist already

subcommands.name: doctor
subcommands.description: Checks for known common issues with pnpm configuration

name: -C
--dir
options.description: Run as if pnpm was started in <path> instead of the current working directory

name: -w
--workspace-root
options.description: Run as if pnpm was started in the root of the <workspace> instead of the current working directory

name: -h
--help
options.description: Output usage information

name: -v
--version
options.description: Show pnpm's version

script: until [[ -f package.json ]] || [[ $PWD = '/' ]]; do cd ..; done; cat package.json
